summary: Cross-chapter audit of cycle 01 manuscript (78,027 prose words, 1,876 prose lines, 18 chapters). REDUNDANCY: Identified 10 character re-introduction findings, 1 setting re-description, 2 proof-of-baseline accumulations, 4 image-density findings, 1 near-verbatim repeated phrase, 17 em-dash density findings (manuscript-wide 654/1876 = 34.9%, threshold 10%), 9 She-opener findings (manuscript-wide 524/5610 = 9.3%, threshold 7%), and 10 hedge-word findings ('something' at 190 occurrences = 1 per 411 words; 'the way' at 130 = 1 per 600 words; threshold 1 per 800). CONSISTENCY: Identified 3 consistency issues — Lena's neural link port material described as 5 different substances across 5 chapters, Margot's feed interface switches between right and left wrist, and Chapter 7 uses double-hyphens while all other chapters use em-dashes. Character ages, timeline, geography, spatial layout, and object tracking are otherwise consistent.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 18 line 3736: 'It ached every afternoon around this time, a low persistent wrongness deep in the bone where the break from February had healed too quickly. The synthetic grafts had knitted the fracture in nine days.'; Ch 17 line 3617: 'The persistent, dull complaint of bone that had been knitted too fast...The wrist had healed in nine days.'; Ch 18 line 3744: 'His right hand picked up the phone. He hadn't decided to pick it up. The motion was the system's — the micro-anticipation of intent.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Kai's wrist ache and proprioceptive gap are re-described in Ch 18 using near-verbatim phrasing from adjacent Ch 17 (both use 'nine days,' 'wrongness,' 'every afternoon'). The wrist ache has been described in Ch 6, 7, 8, 11, 14, 16, 17, and 18 — eight re-descriptions after the Ch 5 crash. The proprioceptive gap has been described six times (Ch 1, 5, 7, 11, 16, 18). Ch 17 line 3617 and Ch 18 line 3736 are adjacent chapters with functionally identical wrist passages.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the wrist-ache paragraph at line 3736 entirely or reduce to a single oblique clause. Replace the proprioceptive-gap passage at line 3744 with a single-sentence reference that shows Kai's evolved relationship to it rather than re-explaining the mechanism.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 18 contains no sentence describing wrist bone knitting speed or the nine-day healing timeline; the proprioceptive-gap concept is referenced in one sentence or fewer without re-explaining the mechanism.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 17 line 3623: 'His system was what Meridian had installed three years ago: the spinal neural bridge, the bilateral synthetic muscle grafts in both legs, the visual processing enhancement. Unpatched.'; Ch 1 lines 33-35 establish the same hardware list; Ch 17 line 3617: 'bone that had been knitted too fast by synthetic grafts...The wrist had healed in nine days' reprises wrist from Ch 7 line 1129, Ch 8 line 1390, Ch 16 line 3330.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 17 re-lists Kai's full enhancement hardware (spinal neural bridge, bilateral leg grafts, visual processing) as if the reader has not already met it in Ch 1. The wrist-ache passage at line 3617 is the seventh re-description. Line 3635 re-describes facial scars already established in Ch 6 line 1004.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Delete the hardware inventory at line 3623 or replace with an oblique reference ('running on the original install, nothing between him and the virus'). Compress the wrist passage to one clause. Cut or compress the facial-scar mention.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 17 does not enumerate spinal bridge, bilateral leg grafts, and visual processing as a list; wrist description is one sentence or fewer.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 16 line 3330: 'His wrist ached on cold mornings, the bone where the break had knitted too fast — a shallow wrongness...He ran paying attention to something most people couldn't feel: the synthetic muscle's autonomous subsystems, the tiny pre-movements in his quadriceps and calves that fired fractionally ahead of his conscious intent.' Established: wrist Ch 5-7; proprioceptive gap Ch 1 lines 31-63, Ch 5 line 745, Ch 7 line 1133, Ch 11 line 2057.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 16 re-describes both Kai's wrist ache (6th re-description) and proprioceptive gap (5th) using extended explanatory prose. Phrasing ('bone where the break had knitted too fast,' 'fired fractionally ahead of his conscious intent') repeats Ch 7 and Ch 11 formulations. This feeds directly into the near-verbatim cluster in Ch 16-17-18.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Compress the wrist reference to a single image. Replace the proprioceptive-gap explanation with a behavioral observation — show what he does with the awareness rather than re-explaining the phenomenon.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 16's Kai passage does not explain what the proprioceptive gap is or how the wrist was injured; both are referenced in one sentence or fewer each.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 7 line 859: 'His hands were steady, the way they always were — an engineer's hands, precise and unhesitating.'; Ch 3 line 395: 'His hands were steady on the interface — they always were, the one part of him that never registered what was happening as strange.'; Ch 1 line 107: 'hands moving with the unhesitating precision of someone who'd done this ten thousand times.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Yusuf's hand steadiness described three times in seven chapters using near-identical vocabulary ('steady,' 'precise,' 'unhesitating,' 'engineer's hands'). Ch 7 line 859 is functionally indistinguishable from Ch 3 line 395. The later inversion at Ch 14 line 2964 ('They were not steady now') works better if the trait has not been hammered.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 7, delete the 'His hands were steady' sentence entirely or replace with a contrasting detail about what is different in this crisis moment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 does not contain the phrase 'hands were steady' or 'engineer's hands' in reference to Yusuf.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 15 line 3185: 'the grey streaks in her hair, the lines around her eyes, the particular fatigue of someone who had been tired for years.'; Ch 4 line 942: 'The grey streaks in her hair seemed more pronounced'; Ch 7 line 1661: 'grey-streaked and usually pinned with architectural precision'; Ch 14 line 2864: 'gray-streaked hair escaping its pin.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Petra's grey-streaked hair is described for the fourth time. The Ch 15 instance additionally re-describes her chronic exhaustion, established in Ch 4 line 942 and Ch 7 line 1661. By Ch 15 the reader has encountered 'grey streaks' three times already.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the hair-color detail in Ch 15 entirely. The scene's power is Petra's institutional authority. If a physical grounding is needed, use a detail not previously established.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 15 does not mention grey streaks or grey-streaked hair in reference to Petra.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 14 line 2864: 'her gray-streaked hair escaping its pin.'; Established Ch 4 line 942; re-described Ch 7 line 1661.
redundancy_findings.problem: Third re-description of Petra's grey-streaked hair. The Ch 14 context is an emergency medical response to Tomas's crash; hair color is the least salient detail.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace with an action-focused descriptor — Petra's competence and speed matter here, not her hair color.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14's description of Petra arriving at Tomas's crash scene does not reference her hair color.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 8 line 1536: 'Tomas was twenty-one and his family ate because of what his body could do.'; Ch 3 line 407: 'Twenty-one, Ecuadorian, first Apex cycle.'; Ch 4 line 612: 'Tomás was twenty-one and eager and came from a family in Quito that needed him to be good.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Tomas's age (twenty-one), Ecuadorian origin, and family financial dependence are re-stated in Ch 8 after being established in Ch 3 and Ch 4. The reader learned this four chapters ago.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the age restatement. Replace the family-dependence sentence with forward-looking narrative pressure about what Tomas risks by signing or not signing.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 8 does not restate Tomas's age as twenty-one or re-explain that his family depends on his athletic income.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 2745: 'It came out as flat observation, which was the way Dara said most things.'; Ch 2 line 244: information 'delivered as information. Nothing in her face suggested that it was strange.'; Ch 13 line 2767: 'her stroke the Bergen program's signature pattern: metronomic, seamlessly paced.'; Ch 2 line 252: 'The Bergen program was different from Meridian's.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Dara's flat affect and Bergen program affiliation are re-described in Ch 13 using nearly identical framing as Ch 2 establishment.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Enter Dara through what Lena now sees differently post-patch rather than re-establishing the baseline. Cut the Bergen program label if the stroke description can stand alone.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 13 does not re-explain Dara's flat affect as a general trait or re-label her stroke as the Bergen program's signature.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 17 line 3551: 'the ambient optimization layer that had been adjusting her stroke pattern, managing her fatigue distribution, smoothing her kick timing since she was four years old — that was silent.'; Ch 16 line 3332: 'The silence in her head where the system's ambient processing had been was becoming, if not comfortable, then at least recognizable.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Lena's post-patch silence is described in adjacent chapters (Ch 16 and Ch 17) using the same framing: the system's ambient processing is now silent, she is adjusting to quiet. Ch 16 establishes this; Ch 17 re-explains at greater length.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the explanatory passage about the optimization layer being silent. Enter the ready-room through the competition context (venue, opponents, stakes). A single reference ('the quiet in her head, familiar now') is sufficient.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 17 does not re-explain what the partial patch does or describe the ambient optimization layer being silent; the post-patch state is referenced in one sentence or fewer.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 line 2727: 'The water was the standard twenty-seven degrees...and the chlorine hit her in the usual way.'; Ch 13 lines 2729-2731: changing in locker room, walking to lane, diving in, underwater acoustics described from scratch; Ch 10 line 1778: 'twenty-seven degrees, a degree warmer than the air'; Ch 2 line 202+: first pool establishment.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 13's pool scene re-inventories the space from scratch: water temperature (27 degrees, matching Ch 10), chlorine, entry ritual (changing, walking to lane, dive), and underwater acoustics (matching Ch 2). The pool was established in Ch 2 and the reader last saw it two chapters earlier in Ch 10.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the temperature callout. Compress the entry-ritual sequence to one sentence. The post-patch sensory contrast ('the water just sounded like water') is the valuable new content; foreground that and strip the re-inventory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 13's pool scene does not state the water temperature numerically or describe the changing-room-to-lane-to-dive entry sequence in more than one sentence.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 13 contains four 'eighteen years' references: line 2599, line 2653 ('eighteen years of calibration sessions'), line 2671 ('eighteen years of being calibrated'), line 2675 ('for eighteen years'). Manuscript-wide, Lena's four-year-old/eighteen-year baseline is proved ~10 times: Ch 2 lines 202, 204, 318; Ch 7 line 1273; Ch 10 lines 1910, 1992; Ch 13 lines 2599, 2653, 2671, 2675; Ch 17 line 3551; Ch 18 line 3806.
redundancy_findings.problem: The fact that Lena has been enhanced since age four / for eighteen years is stated ~10 times across the manuscript, with a dense cluster of four instances in Ch 13 alone. While the Ch 13 patch-installation context provides new leverage, four separate 'eighteen years' callouts within a single chapter exceeds what the reader needs.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the strongest Ch 13 instance (line 2653, 'Your system's been learning how you swim since you were four') which provides structural consequence. Cut or vary the other three instances within Ch 13. Manuscript-wide, retain 4-5 total instances and cut the rest.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 13 contains no more than two references to the eighteen-year / since-age-four timeline.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 7 lines 1149, 1183, 1195, 1225, 1251: five uses of 'institutional' in one chapter — 'institutional persistence' (coffee), 'institutional coffee' (dialogue), 'institutional table' (description), 'institutional broccoli' (food), 'institutional stuff' (coffee again). Manuscript-wide: 'institutional' appears 24 times across 13 chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: The word 'institutional' appears five times in Ch 7, a chapter that is a quiet cafeteria conversation. The reader has already absorbed the facility's institutional character from Ch 1 and Ch 3. Five instances in one chapter turns atmosphere into a verbal tic. Manuscript-wide, 24 uses of 'institutional' exceed what this descriptor needs to do.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one 'institutional' reference in Ch 7 (the strongest atmospheric beat). Replace the other four with specific sensory detail that evokes institutional atmosphere without the word itself. Manuscript-wide, target 8-10 retained instances.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 contains no more than one instance of the word 'institutional.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 6 contains 5 'fluorescent' instances: line 845 (exam table lighting), line 942 (Petra's hair under fluorescent), line 1016 (wrist brace catching light), line 1036 ('this fluorescent room'), line 1082 (corridor fluorescent buzz), line 1102 (bathroom fluorescent flicker). Manuscript-wide: 'fluorescent' appears 40 times across 16 chapters. Not tracked as a motif in outline/outline.md.
redundancy_findings.problem: 'Fluorescent' appears 40 times manuscript-wide (target: keep 8-10 for an image at 20+). Ch 6 is the densest cluster with 5-6 instances in a single chapter. The word has become a default lighting descriptor applied to every indoor scene rather than a meaningful sensory detail.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch 6, keep the single most atmospherically effective fluorescent reference and cut or replace the rest with varied lighting descriptors (flat white overhead, strip lighting, the cold buzz of tube lights). Apply same principle manuscript-wide.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 6 contains no more than two instances of the word 'fluorescent.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 9 contains 3 'fluorescent' instances: lines 1651, 1723, 1757. Manuscript-wide total: 40 (target: keep 8-10).
redundancy_findings.problem: Three fluorescent references in Ch 9, contributing to the manuscript-wide 40-instance overuse. The calibration bay's lighting does not need to be re-established each time Yusuf works in it.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one fluorescent reference in Ch 9 (preferably the mismatched-ballast detail at line 1651 which is character-specific). Cut or replace the others.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 9 contains no more than one instance of 'fluorescent.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 15 lines 3081, 3185, 3324: three 'fluorescent' instances. The chapter also uses fluorescent as a character-judgment device twice (Margot's mirror, Petra's aging). Manuscript-wide total: 40.
redundancy_findings.problem: Three fluorescent references in Ch 15. The reader is 15 chapters into a facility-set novel and no longer needs fluorescent lighting re-established.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep at most one; the mirror scene (line 3081) where Margot confronts her reflection is the strongest. Cut the other two.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 15 contains no more than one instance of 'fluorescent.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 7 lines 1165, 1195, 1369: three 'fluorescent' instances. Also Ch 7 contains 5 'institutional' uses (see R012). Combined, the chapter deploys 8 institutional/fluorescent descriptors.
redundancy_findings.problem: Three fluorescent references in Ch 7, stacked on top of five 'institutional' uses. The cafeteria scene is over-described with facility-atmosphere signifiers.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one fluorescent reference in Ch 7. Combined with the R012 institutional reduction, the chapter's atmosphere should be conveyed through character behavior and dialogue rather than repeated setting adjectives.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 contains no more than one instance of 'fluorescent.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: The 10-word phrase 'the neural link port at the base of her skull' appears verbatim across Ch 7 (line 1195), Ch 10 (line 1768, 1910), Ch 11 (line 2047), Ch 12 (line 2324), Ch 16 (line 3466, 3496), and Ch 17 (line 3551). Variants with slight wording changes appear in Ch 6 (line 1032) and Ch 18 (line 3804). Total: 9+ near-identical descriptions of the same object.
redundancy_findings.problem: The neural link port phrase is a repeated scene-entry template: each chapter where Lena appears re-describes the port at the base of her skull as though introducing it for the first time. By Ch 12, the reader has encountered this phrase or a close variant at least 5 times. The repetition creates a formulaic re-entry pattern.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: After the Ch 6 establishment (line 1032), subsequent chapters should reference the port without re-describing its location or physical characteristics. Use 'the port,' 'the hardware,' or a character-specific gesture ('she touched the back of her neck') instead of the full descriptive phrase. Retain the full description for at most one later scene where the physical detail matters narratively (e.g., Ch 13 patch installation).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The phrase 'neural link port at the base of her skull' appears in no more than 3 chapters total (establishment + 2 later instances where physical detail is plot-relevant).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 17: 46/92 prose lines contain em-dashes = 50.0%. Manuscript-wide: 654/1876 = 34.9% (threshold 10%, target <8%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 17 has em-dash density of 50.0%, far exceeding the 10% threshold. The em-dash is used for parenthetical asides, appositives, and mid-sentence pivots at a rate that flattens the prose rhythm. Manuscript target: reduce from 654 to <150 em-dash lines.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines in Ch 17 from 46 to ~7 (39 cuts). Convert parenthetical em-dash asides to commas, semicolons, colons, periods, or restructured sentences. Reserve em-dashes for their highest-impact uses: dramatic interruptions, voice-specific rhythms, and moments where the break creates meaning.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 17 contains no more than 7 prose lines with em-dashes (≤~8% of 92 prose lines).

Reserve em-dashes for their highest-impact uses: dramatic interruptions, voice-specific rhythms, and moments where the break creates meaning.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 14 contains no more than 10 prose lines with em-dashes (≤~8% of 123 prose lines).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 9: 33/102 prose lines contain em-dashes = 32.4%. Manuscript-wide: 654/1876 = 34.9% (threshold 10%, target <8%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 9 has em-dash density of 32.4%, far exceeding the 10% threshold. The em-dash is used for parenthetical asides, appositives, and mid-sentence pivots at a rate that flattens the prose rhythm. Manuscript target: reduce from 654 to <150 em-dash lines.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash-containing lines in Ch 9 from 33 to ~8 (25 cuts). Convert parenthetical em-dash asides to commas, semicolons, colons, periods, or restructured sentences. Reserve em-dashes for their highest-impact uses: dramatic interruptions, voice-specific rhythms, and moments where the break creates meaning.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 9 contains no more than 8 prose lines with em-dashes (≤~8% of 102 prose lines).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 2: 68/279 sentences begin with 'She' = 24.4%. Manuscript-wide: 524/5610 = 9.3% (threshold 7%).
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch 2 has 'She'-opener density of 24.4%, well above the 7% threshold. This creates a rhythmic monotony where consecutive sentences beginning with 'She' flatten the prose into a subject-verb march.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'She'-opener sentences in Ch 2 from 68 to ~19 (49 rewrites). Vary sentence openings: lead with setting, sensory detail, dependent clauses, object-first constructions, or dialogue attributions.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 2 has no more than 19 sentences (≤7% of ~279) beginning with 'She.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 5: 'something' appears 18 times in 4037 words = 1 per 224 words. Manuscript-wide: 190 occurrences in 78,027 words = 1 per 411 words (threshold: 1 per 800).
redundancy_findings.problem: 'Something' appears 18 times in Ch 5, a rate of 1 per 224 words. This vague pronoun substitutes for specific imagery. Manuscript-wide target: reduce from 190 to ~98 occurrences.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'something' in Ch 5 from 18 to ~5 (13 cuts). Replace each cut with the specific noun, sensation, or concept the 'something' stands in for. Keep 'something' only where vagueness is the point (character genuinely cannot name the experience).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 5 contains no more than 5 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch 9: 'the way' appears 17 times in 4016 words = 1 per 236 words. Manuscript-wide: 130 occurrences in 78,027 words = 1 per 600 words (threshold: 1 per 800).
redundancy_findings.problem: 'The way' appears 17 times in Ch 9, a rate of 1 per 236 words. This construction ('the way she said most things,' 'the way you'd reach for a phone') becomes a habitual sentence shape rather than a deliberate rhetorical choice. Manuscript-wide target: reduce from 130 to ~98.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce 'the way' in Ch 9 from 17 to ~5 (12 cuts). Replace with direct comparison, metaphor, or restructured syntax. Keep 'the way' only where the simile structure is genuinely the most effective formulation.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 9 contains no more than 5 instances of 'the way.'

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 6 line 1032: 'two centimeters of surgical polymer'; Ch 10 line 1910: 'The implant's ceramic edge'; Ch 12 line 2324: 'a small circle of dark composite'; Ch 13 line 2581: 'two-centimeter circle of metal and ceramic'; Ch 18 line 3804: 'the small circle of titanium.'
consistency_findings.problem: Lena's neural link port material is described as five different substances across five chapters: surgical polymer (Ch 6), ceramic (Ch 10), dark composite (Ch 12), metal and ceramic (Ch 13), and titanium (Ch 18). These are mutually contradictory — a single implant cannot be made of all five materials.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Choose one canonical material description (e.g., 'titanium and ceramic composite' would reconcile most variants) and standardize across all five chapters. Update Ch 6 line 1032, Ch 10 line 1910, Ch 12 line 2324, Ch 13 line 2581, and Ch 18 line 3804 to use the same material.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Every description of Lena's neural link port material uses the same term throughout the manuscript.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 2 line 262: 'her feed interface on her right wrist'; Ch 10 line 1770: 'Margot strapped the feed interface to her left wrist'; Ch 12 line 2302: 'Margot's left hand had gone to her right wrist — to the feed interface' (right wrist); Ch 12 line 2360: 'the feed interface on her left wrist' (left wrist).
consistency_findings.problem: Margot's feed interface switches wrists: right (Ch 2 line 262, Ch 12 line 2302), left (Ch 10 line 1770, Ch 12 line 2360). Ch 12 contradicts itself within the same chapter — line 2302 places it on the right wrist while line 2360 places it on the left. The device is personal equipment Margot wears consistently; it should be on the same wrist throughout.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Choose one canonical wrist (right, per the Ch 2 establishment) and correct all references. Update Ch 10 line 1770 and Ch 12 line 2360 from 'left' to 'right.' Verify Ch 12 line 2302 reads consistently.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Every reference to which wrist Margot's feed interface is on names the same wrist throughout the manuscript.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch 7 (lines 1127-1383) uses double-hyphens with spaces (' -- ') in 35 instances as em-dash substitutes. All other 17 chapters use proper em-dashes (—). Example: Ch 7 line 1129: 'The bone had knitted -- faster than any break had a right to mend.'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch 7 is the only chapter using double-hyphens instead of em-dashes. This creates a visible formatting inconsistency when the manuscript is read as a compiled document. All 35 instances are in Ch 7; no other chapter exhibits this pattern.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Convert all 35 double-hyphen instances in Ch 7 from ' -- ' to '—' (em-dash) to match the rest of the manuscript.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch 7 contains no double-hyphen sequences (' -- '); all dashes in Ch 7 are em-dashes (—) matching the other 17 chapters.
